title: Share of population living in extreme poverty vs. mean income or consumption per day
subtitle: Extreme poverty is defined as living below the [International Poverty Line](#dod:international-poverty-line) of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: This data is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices. Depending on the country and year, it relates to income (measured after taxes and benefits) or to consumption, [per capita](#dod:per-capita).
caption:
Share of population living in extreme poverty vs. mean income or consumption per day

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot visualizes the relationship between the share of the population living in extreme poverty and the average annual income or consumption per day for the year 2025. The vertical axis represents the share in extreme poverty, ranging from 0% to over 80%, while the horizontal axis displays average annual income or consumption in international dollars at 2021 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale from $1,000 to $20,000. Each circle represents a country, with its size corresponding to the total population. The data points are color-coded by region: Africa in purple, Asia in teal, South America in brown, North America in orange, Europe in blue, and Oceania in light blue.

A clear negative correlation is visible across the chart. Countries with lower average incomes, situated on the left side of the x-axis between $1,000 and $2,000, exhibit the highest shares of extreme poverty. The Democratic Republic of Congo appears at the top left with a share exceeding 80%, followed by Madagascar and Niger, which sit between 60% and 75%. Nigeria and Mali cluster in the 40% range. As average income increases along the horizontal axis, the share of the population in extreme poverty generally declines. India and Pakistan, represented by large teal circles, are positioned near the $2,000 mark with poverty shares around 20% to 25%. Further to the right, countries like China, Brazil, and Turkey show significantly lower poverty rates, hovering near or below 5%, with average incomes ranging from roughly $6,000 to $12,000. The United States and other high-income nations appear on the far right, beyond the $20,000 mark, with poverty shares close to 0%. The visual distribution demonstrates that higher mean income or consumption levels correspond to lower proportions of the population living below the $3 per day threshold.
